steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence lists three factors after "namely." Two of those items contain internal commas ("their velocity upon entering Earth's mesosphere, with higher-velocity meteoroids releasing more energy" and "atmospheric fluctuations, such as changes in humidity"). When list items contain internal commas, Standard English uses semicolons to separate the main items so readers can tell where one factor ends and the next begins.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — em dash: An em dash signals elaboration or a sharp break, not a list separator. ✗
  • Choice B — comma: Using a comma here blurs the boundary between the first and second factors because commas already appear inside the second and third items. ✗
  • Choice C — semicolon: Correctly separates the first factor from the second in a complex list, distinguishing the main items from the internal commas within them. ✓
  • Choice D — period: A period would end the sentence mid-list, cutting off the second and third factors entirely. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
